Backtesting, in this case, may not be highly useful, but it does give some rough idea of how an HG portfolio (as described in this article) should perform in comparison to our benchmarks, the S&P 500 and Vanguard Dividend Appreciation Index Fund ETF (VIG). We are presenting the backtesting from January 2020 until January 2026. In fact, the period is rather short for any meaningful backtesting, but some of the stocks do not have a long history. The assumption is that our model portfolio was invested equally in ten stocks and rebalanced every year.

It is almost certain that we would not have picked the same set of stocks 10 years or even 5 years ago. So, this is more of an academic exercise and should be viewed with a grain of salt. Also, past performance is no guarantee of any kind for future returns.

Chart-1:

Performance Since Jan. 2023

This series was started in January 2023. Since then, we have provided the progress and performance of the series as it progressed. We compare the performance of the portfolio with the benchmark fund, Vanguard Dividend Appreciation ETF, and also with the S&P 500. We provide the cumulative figures from the start date of January 2023 until the current month. Obviously, the performance will vary from month to month. Also, the performance of benchmarks may not match exactly with a buy-and-hold portfolio, as we take the performance from a specific start date and end date every month to match our 10-HG portfolio.

Table-3A:

Concluding Remarks:

This past month, the performance of our HG-portfolio slowed down quite a bit with a 3.27% return for the month, though it still compared well with the negative return of the S&P 500 during this period. Also, the portfolio has completed 37 months (or 3 years plus), and the portfolio has returned close to 29% on an annualized basis, while the S&P 500 returned 19% annualized in the same period. On a cumulative basis, the performance was 119% versus 71%, respectively.

This month's performance was pulled down mainly by a 27% decline in Expedia (EXPE). The main reason was the big drop in the valuations of the software companies due to the fear of AI impacting the software businesses. The selling was across the segment, and every company in the software segment was impacted. Our portfolio performance would have been roughly 6% excluding EXPE. Nonetheless, on the positive side, the star performers for this past month were Comfort Systems (FIX), Howmet Aerospace (HWM), and Grupo Cibest S.A (CIB), with 28%, 13.6%, and 8.5% gains, respectively. The semiconductor company Lam Research (LRCX) also performed well with nearly 7% gains.

This month, we are retaining only four stocks from the previous month and replacing six stocks. However, some of the changes are of a minor nature. For example, the gold mining company AngloGold Ashanti (AU) is replacing Kinross Gold (KGC), even though both could be equally good. Also, electronic components company Vertiv (VRT) is replacing Amphenol (APH), as APH appears to be losing momentum. Also, the travel booking company, Expedia (EXPE), is being dropped. As can be seen in Table-3, the final portfolio of 10 stocks is highly diversified into many different sectors and industry segments.

Please note that our list changes from month to month. These are not "buy" recommendations as such, but a short list of outstanding dividend-growth stocks to research further.

The stocks selected in this series, even though they are excellent choices, are still subject to market volatility. Since these are high-growth stocks, we should expect them to have high volatility as well, especially when the market appears to be overvalued. We recommend due diligence.

data.title: VICI Properties: Conquer Growing Dividends
data.description:
Co-authored by Kody's Dividends

After the Battle of Zela, Julius Caesar famously wrote in his letter to the Roman Senate

Veni, vidi, vici.

This simply translates to "I came, I saw, I conquered."

As a long-term dividend investor focused on both capital gains from my holdings as well as dividend growth, this is the kind of description I want to have for my portfolio and its performance. To do so, I must have holdings that provide me with reliable growing dividends and trade at an attractive value.

When it comes to investing in real estate through real estate investment trusts (REITs), many focus on different niches. You can go out and buy REITs that focus on billboards or ones that focus on office buildings.

I want to take a look at an investment opportunity within the REIT sector that focuses on experiential properties. These are the types of properties that you go to for an experience, not just to buy something or to go to work. Even when the economy is struggling, individuals will still find ways to experience something new. It may not be a trip to Disney (DIS) every single year, but it's still a focus of many.

Today, I want to look at an excellent investment in this REIT sub-sector.

Let's dive in!

Well-Positioned For The Experience Economy

A survey conducted by Empower a few years ago found that 88% of Americans say that their love of life comes from experiences. Just as strikingly, 86% of Gen Z noted that they overspent on live events, citing fear of missing out as a top reason.

In the world of experiences, VICI Properties' (VICI) real estate is unparalleled. The company's properties include iconic Las Vegas properties, such as Caesars Palace Las Vegas, MGM Grand Las Vegas, and The Venetian Resort Las Vegas. Key properties outside of Las Vegas include the Borgata Hotel Casino in Atlantic City and MGM Grand Detroit.

VICI's business model is strong, too. The company's weighted-average lease term is approximately 40 years. Around 42% of its rent roll included CPI-linked escalators in 2025, with this set to escalate to 90% by 2035 (with applicable caps). Even during the COVID-19 pandemic, when its operators were shut down for months, VICI collected 100% of its rent.

Moving forward, the REIT should be able to steadily grow AFFO per share. This is because, in addition to its lease escalators, Kody noted the recently announced Golden Entertainment acquisition in his December article.

The $1.16 billion deal will add $87 million of initial annual cash rent (with a 30-year initial lease term and four five-year renewal options). That equates to a 7.5% cap rate. Starting in the third year of the lease, annual rent escalation will also be 2%. The tenant also covers its rent almost twice over, which points to the stability of the deal expected to close in mid-2026.

As a result, the FAST Graphs analyst consensus is for AFFO per share to rise by 3.9% annually through 2028, off an anticipated 2025 base of $2.37.

VICI's net leverage ratio of 5.0x is well within its targeted range of between 5.0x and 5.5x. This earns it a BBB- S&P credit rating with a stable outlook.

VICI's 6.2% dividend yield looks to be secure. The AFFO payout ratio is positioned to be in the mid-70% range for 2026. That gives it the flexibility to hand out more 4% annual dividend raises, which equates to a Chowder number above 10.

VICI is a compelling value, too. From the current $29.72 share price, the stock is priced at a forward 12-month P/AFFO ratio of 11.7 (90% weight for 2026 and 10% weight for 2027). This is significantly under the FAST Graphs seven-year average P/AFFO ratio of 15.7.

Again, we don't envision VICI returning to such a multiple anytime soon. The interest rate environment will put too much pressure on the valuation to let that happen under most circumstances.

However, VICI's growth prospects are reasonably intact, with a nearly 4% annual AFFO per share growth outlook over the next few years. In our view, that could support a fair value P/AFFO multiple of 14.1 (one standard deviation less than the seven-year average). This suggests VICI's shares are worth nearly $35 each, which would be a 17% discount to our fair value estimate.

If the REIT returns to fair value and meets growth expectations, it could deliver a 26% upside between now and the end of 2026. Through 2030, VICI could post 12% annual total returns.

As Kody highlighted in December, some relevant risks facing the experiential REIT include tenant concentration and declining Las Vegas visitor volumes.

Last week, it was reported by the Nevada Gaming Control Board that statewide gaming win was down 6.1% year-over-year on the Las Vegas Strip in December.

Even though fewer people came to Las Vegas in 2025, Nevada still set a record for gaming revenue at $15.8 billion. This supports our thesis that Las Vegas is becoming a higher end market, emerging as the go-to destination in the sports entertainment world.

That alleviates these concerns to a degree. However, VICI's high dependence on the Las Vegas Strip still makes this a risk worth closely watching.

What's more, tenant concentration remains a key risk. Nearly three-quarters (74%) of VICI's ABR came from Caesars Entertainment (CZR) and MGM Resorts (MGM). If the former can't turn the ship around soon, regional rent concessions could put a modest dent in VICI's AFFO per share. That would set its AFFO per share back a year or possibly two years.

Conclusion

When it comes to being a landlord, I often love to utilize the REIT sector because it allows me to enjoy a wide-ranging exposure to different properties without having to have millions of dollars or billions of dollars to put into building a portfolio. I can buy shares of VICI, Realty Income (O), and Rexford Industrial (REXR) to enjoy a wide-ranging exposure to property types, all providing me with steady, strong income streams that are growing.

As a dividend investor, my focus is to have a fortress-quality portfolio. Within Dividend Kings, our Model Portfolio is a fortress-quality portfolio. To do this, you must focus first on quality, next on valuation, and finally on successful dividend growth. This three-layer approach allows you to benefit tremendously from the movements of the market and from dividends pouring into your account.

Filter out the market's noise to get out of the chaos of day-to-day trading and into the clarity of Dividend King royalty life.

data.title: Himalaya Shipping: I Missed The Boat - Upgrade To Buy
data.description:
Himalaya Shipping Ltd. (HSHP) owns and operates 12 Newcastlemax/Capesize dry bulk ships. I covered the stock some time ago. However, my thesis did not turn out as I expected, following a total return of over 70% despite my "Sell" rating.

I had rated it a sell due to its high leverage, causing high fleet breakevens and an underestimation of the capesize market. Given that my rate outlook for capesize has changed, I naturally have to change my rating to reflect that, which is why I am upgrading the company to "Buy" despite my previous call.

Debt Concern Was Overstated

On the question of debt, I had previously stated that the company had large debt loads, leading to a large interest payment for the company, increasing the breakeven cost significantly.

Upon closer examination, when taking a look at last quarter's reported net interest expense, we can see that it is $12.4 million per quarter. Considering the fleet consists of 12 vessels, this gives us ownership days of 1,095 across the fleet in Q4. This gives us a daily interest expense of $11,324.

Adding this to the OPEX of the company, which sits at approximately $7 million per quarter or $6,393 daily per vessel, gives us a breakeven point of $17,717 daily per vessel. Naturally, I will not include a dry dock provision due to the fleet consisting of newbuilds.

This is significantly higher than its closest peer, Seanergy Maritime (SHIP), which has a daily breakeven of $10,835.

Nevertheless, one must also consider the fact that this fleet is completely new and will therefore earn a premium over the capesize index due to better efficiency and larger size.

Premium Over The Index

Likely the most important part as to why I was previously wrong is due to the fleet earning a significant premium over the index. How significant? According to this press release, the capesize index averaged $28,875 daily, while the company earned an average of $39,600 a day. This underscores an outperformance of 37%.

Additionally, the company has historically achieved an outperformance over the index of 48%, according to their Q4 2025 investor presentation.

The premium is a function of the ships all being equipped with scrubbers, high-efficiency dual fuel engines, as well as their larger cargo sizes (210,000 DWT) compared to standard capesizes.

Capesize Supply Dynamics

Supply remains favourable despite a slight uptick in the capesize segments from around 9% to 12%. Below is an extract from the investor presentation outlining the current order book of the capesize class.

On top of that, some speculate that regulatory pressures will cause true supply to stay stagnant or even decline since 32% of all capesize vessels are over 15 years old, which would be forced to adopt measures such as slower sailing speeds, efficiency upgrades, and even scrapping.

The Demand Picture

The most important aspect in capesize cargo demand is China, specifically its economic and industrial health. In this segment, there is iron, which is the main demand driver at 70% of all volumes. The rest of the demand is picked up by the 2 supporting goods, which are coal and bauxite.

As some of you might already know, the property crisis in China has weighed on domestic steel demand, causing a downstream effect on the main input goods for steel, which are iron and, to a lesser extent, coal. While exports have served as a release valve for excess supply, this is not enough to support the steel industry.

While demand for bauxite has been stronger due to aluminium's various uses in industry, that is more than offset by coal's weakness. Therefore, for there to be stronger shipment volumes in aggregate, there must be a rebound in the steel sector. Therefore, the property and construction sector in China should be monitored for any signs of recovery.

Despite all of this, the distances that ships will be travelling to transport iron are only getting larger due to tailwinds such as the Simandou project, which has recently begun exports and is expected to reroute the shorter haul route Australia-China to the long haul Guinea-China.

The reason why this project will reroute said trade is due to the fact that Simandou's iron ore quality is higher, as well as being a good way for China to diversify from the geopolitical risk of relying on one supplier.

Overall, the project's rerouting effect more than offsets weak potential volume and will, in my opinion, drive more tonne-mile demand in aggregate.

Immediate Capital Return

An interesting management choice by the company is to return most of its earnings to shareholders through a monthly dividend rather than paying down debt or investing in new vessels. This naturally drives significant returns in a strong rate environment such as the one we have recently been experiencing.

This approach can have weaknesses, as management is focusing on an aggressive capital return policy rather than building up cash for a 'rainy day,' which can lead to share issuances in weak rate environments, such as on March 21st, 2025, according to this filing.

Nevertheless, I overstated this in my last article, as the company has returned $26.9 million in dividends over the past twelve months while only diluting by $15.5 million over the same period. Additionally, due to my bullish view on rates and the improvement of the company's cash position to $32.4, we can expect dividends to continue.

Valuation

To evaluate the company, I will assume two scenarios. We will assume average rates of $19,275 going forward, which assumes a 33% contraction when compared to the Capesize T/C rates at the time of writing ($28,769 a day according to Fearnleys). This takes into account any black swans.

Otherwise, we will assume a $31,645 per day rate, which represents a growth of 10% due to my overall bullishness on the sector and Q1 typically being seasonally weak.

To be conservative, I will apply a premium to the index of only 30% going forward, which means that the worst-case scenario will achieve $25,057 a day and the normal scenario will achieve $41,138. We will assume a 100% utilization rate due to the desirability of the new vessels compared to the vast majority of other vessels. We will be assuming a fleet breakeven of $17,717 daily (as calculated earlier), I have made a table below to calculate profitability:

Scenario Worst-Case Normal
Adjusted Rates 25,057 41,138
Fleet Breakeven 17,717 17,717
Daily Fleet Profit (Loss) 7,340 23,421
Operating Days 4,380 4,380
Yearly Profit (in millions) 32.15 102.58

Deducting from the above numbers $5 million a year in SG&A, we are left with $27.15 million in the worst-case scenario and $97.58 million in the normal scenario.

I consider the normal scenario as the only intellectually honest way to value the company, as the worst-case scenario has (in my view) a low chance of actually occurring, but it is good to know the company would break even either way.

With the cash-based profit of $97.58 million, this represents cash flow of 2.09 per share based on 46.7 shares outstanding.

Naturally, this company can be viewed as a call option on Capesize rates, meaning that if they spike, so do the earnings. However, if rates remain the same, you can expect a good yield based on the latest share price of 13.50

Risks and Catalysts

The main risk is the same as the main catalyst, that being China. If China goes into a recession, expect the worst-case scenario to take place regarding rates. Readers are advised to monitor Chinese economic growth and industrial health, as this is paramount for steel demand.

Naturally the main catalyst would be any stimulus measures or recovery in the Chinese property and construction sector. Readers are to closely monitor news regarding government policy shifts, particularly regarding property.

Overall Takeaway

At the end of the day, the company is not as bad as I thought. On the contrary, it was a very strong pick, which I mistakenly judged based on shortsightedness. Upon further research, I rate this stock a "buy" and set my price target to 16.00, which represents a 13% cash flow yield with more potential for upside depending on rates. My price target is at a premium of 17% compared to the current share price at the time of writing.

data.title: The Supreme Court May Have Just Prevented A Recession
data.description:
February 20th was a really fascinating day for market participants. For months now, it had been anticipated that the US Supreme Court would eventually rule on the tariffs imposed by Donald Trump under the IEEPA (International Emergency Economic Powers Act). And finally, the results came out. The decision by the court was that the implementation of the tariffs under this act was unconstitutional. This immediately wipes the slate clean when it comes to tariff implementation. But that doesn't mean that the doors to this regressive tax policy are closed. In fact, shortly after the news broke, Donald Trump revealed new tariffs under another act. And it is likely that while these existing tariffs must be temporary, newer tariffs under other aspects of the law will be attempted.

I always do my best to keep political views outside of my investing analysis. I strip away the biases and look at the substance. And in fact, there are political views from both sides of the aisle that I support, as well as views on both sides that I disagree with. So with that in mind, I can say that I am quite critical of broad-based tariffs. Their regressive nature, combined with other government actions such as those reducing health care access and reducing government employment, risks pushing the US into a recession. In fact, starting in August of last year, I said that a recession is all but guaranteed.

When I first heard the news that the US Supreme Court had given the axe to these tariffs, I was initially elated. But the subsequent decision by the administration to implement new ones means that my overall assessment of the economy is, at least for now, unchanged. Based on all the data that I have seen, we are heading toward a recession. And I believe that investors would be wise to act accordingly.

A fascinating development

I was probably as surprised as anyone when the news scrolled across my screen saying that the US Supreme Court ruled that tariffs under the IEEPA were unconstitutional. I wasn't surprised at the decision so much as I was at the timing. I had figured it would have been announced later in the year. For context, starting last year, shortly after he came into office again, Donald Trump started imposing tariffs on various countries, invoking the IEEPA and the authority he believed it grants the President. Under the act, the President is granted the authority to 'investigate, block during the pendency of an investigation, regulate, direct and compel, nullify, void, prevent, or prohibit… importation or exportation.’

Under the current administration, the belief was that this language allowed the President to impose tariffs since they dealt directly with imports and exports. The ruling, however, points out that there is no mention of tariffs or duties in the act at all. The assertion, then, was that if Congress had intended to convey the power to impose tariffs to the Executive branch, it would have been explicit in the act in the way that it has been explicit in tariff statutes. Whether or not you agree with the imposition of tariffs, I think that this logic is sound, though I would say that I can also understand why some would push back since the word ‘regulate’ could be construed to mean 'to impose tariffs.' But at the end of the day, absent an explicit mention in another act, only Congress has the power to impose taxes on the American people or companies. And make no mistake. Tariffs are, in fact, a tax on the American public.

This is not my opinion. In fact, the Federal Reserve recently said as much. In their own study, published on February 12th of this year, they looked at the effective impact that tariffs have had on the American people. As the image above illustrates, in the first eight months of 2025, importers, which would be those that received the imports, absorbed 94% of the increase in prices caused by tariffs. It is noteworthy, however, that as the year progressed, this rate did drop. By November, it was down to 86%. This decline can almost certainly be attributable to the fact that companies became nimbler. They reorganized their supply chains and almost certainly intensified negotiations with foreign product providers. We do see in the image below, as an example, that compared to 2017 and 2024, the percentage of import value changed significantly in some respects. The US saw the share of imports coming from China drop considerably from around 24% in 2017 to about 9% last year. Meanwhile, other countries have seen exposure increase. Mexico and Vietnam are two such examples.

The big question is what happens next. If the tariff matter was going to be dropped entirely, I would become much more neutral on the economy. But there are actions being taken by the current Administration that will leave in place, at least for now, some tariffs. In a public statement that was made following the decision, Donald Trump said that he was invoking Section 122 of the Trade Act of 1974. Under this section, when there are 'large and serious balance-of-payments deficits’ impacting the US, the President has the authority to impose tariffs of up to 15% for a span of no more than 150 days. That 150-day mark has been initiated, though the tariff rate has been capped at 10% globally.

For countries such as China, as well as others that were severely negatively impacted by much higher tariffs, this is a win. It's also noteworthy that unless Congress acts, the 150-day mark is a hard limit under Section 122. From my understanding, it cannot be rolled over into a subsequent 150-day window of time. For some, this might seem like the end of the tariff drama. But there are other avenues that the Administration has that it can implement in order to achieve its goals.

Under Section 232 of the Trade Expansion Act of 1962, tariffs can be imposed by the President. However, before that occurs, the Secretary of Commerce must conduct an investigation. It must then be determined whether imports threatened to impair national security. And in the event that they do, the President has the authority to impose tariffs or quotas, or to adjust imports using other mechanisms. Seeing as how this avenue has no explicit tariff cap and can technically last indefinitely, this is a serious option that's on the table. But it will take time. On top of this, any sort of investigation and report that would conclude that sweeping tariffs are appropriate will face intense pushback from Congress and also risks being taken to the Supreme Court. It is worth noting that steel and aluminum tariffs have, in the past, been imposed under this authorization.

There's also the Trade Act of 1974. Under Section 301, the US Trade Representative can initiate an investigation. If the conclusion of that investigation is that a specific foreign country is engaging in unfair practices, then the President can impose tariffs or take other retaliatory trade-related actions. The tariffs that were imposed upon China in 2018 would be one successful example of this implementation. And then we also have Section 201 of the Trade Act of 1974. Through this section, the US International Trade Commission must initiate an investigation. This would be in response to alleged injuries from global competition or a sudden surge of imports. If it determines that a serious injury has been incurred, then tariffs can be imposed on a temporary basis. There can also be other actions, such as quotas. But this is not a short-term action necessarily. Even though it is limited in time, it's typically capped at four years, with the option to extend that longer. Solar panel tariffs imposed back in 2018 fell under this category.

The most likely of these paths, in my opinion, would be none other than Section 232. This seems to me, as somebody who is not a lawyer, to be more subjective in terms of determination. The argument could be made, though I think it would fail under intense scrutiny, that national security will be impacted in a negative way by a persistent trade deficit and, if the government sticks with its previous rhetoric, drug trafficking from certain nations. If I had to guess, I would say that before this 150-day window ends, this would be the second, and that will ultimately be imposed or at least attempted. If it fails, I would say that the chance of the other two sections working out would be very close to zero.

The economy is looking worse

The COVID-19 pandemic might seem dead and buried by now. But its effects continue to ripple through this country in a way that's not too different from how 9/11 did. At the start of the pandemic, the economy contracted. Job losses mounted, and lifestyles changed drastically. Nobody, not even myself, anticipated that we would see the economy rebound as quickly as we ultimately did once things started dying down. This caused significant supply chain issues. It didn't help that government stimulus under both the Trump and Biden administrations caused significant amounts of capital to be injected into the economy. The end result of these disruptions, combined with low-interest rates and quantitative easing, was a surge in asset prices. The median home price exploded, and inflation hit multi-decade highs.

Although these conditions were challenging, the Federal Reserve acted quickly. Certain government actions also played a role in helping to bring down inflation faster than what we saw in any other major country on the planet. But the end result was high-interest rates that have caused the housing market to struggle in recent years and plunging consumer confidence. Speaking of consumer confidence, in the chart above, you can see that, right now, it's near all-time lows. Just as it seemed as though the worst was over and that declining interest rates would be the new normal, tariffs were implemented. This created inflationary pressures that proved harmful for the American people.

According to the Tax Foundation, in 2025, tariffs imposed since the start of that year amounted to an average tax increase per US household of about $1,000. Most of this impact came from the tariffs implemented under the IEEPA. But not all of them. For instance, there were some tariffs imposed under Section 232. According to that source, the remaining new tariffs under Section 232 will still cost the average household in the US about $400 this year. That is still friction at a time when consumers cannot afford it. But at least it does mark a meaningful improvement.

These tariffs will unfortunately weaken GDP moving forward. In fact, the expectation is that it will create a permanent drag on GDP of about 0.2% per year. This does not even include impacts associated with retaliation from other nations. Yes, it will raise around $490 billion of tax revenue over the next decade. However, as we discussed already, this is money coming largely out of the pockets of American companies and consumers.

This impact alone would not be enough, in my opinion, to risk a recession if it were not for the fact that the US economy is already on shaky ground. Take jobs data as an example. As the chart above illustrates, the total number of jobs in the US is growing slower now than it was in previous years. The most recent month that we have data available for would be January of 2026. The total number of non-farm jobs amounted to 158.63 million. A year earlier, it was 158.27 million. That's an increase over the last 12 months of just 359,000 jobs. This actually matches up quite well with some other data. Originally, it was estimated that the US economy added 584,000 jobs for all of last year. But that number has been revised lower by the US Bureau of Labor Statistics to just 181,000. This is a much more important indicator, in my view, than the January jobs figure that showed robust growth of 130,000 jobs. After all, revised data has the benefit of additional inputs that went into it.

Taking the January to January data for the last several years, we can see in the table above just how many jobs have been added or lost over time. For instance, from January of 2024 through January of 2025, the US economy added 1.24 million jobs. The year before that, the addition was 2.26 million. Admittedly, there were certain disruptions, mostly starting in 2020 and extending through at least 2023. The rapid loss of jobs because of the COVID-19 pandemic, followed by a recovery, certainly created some comparability issues. But even if we look at prior years, we don't see any time under which the jobs picture has been this bad outside of the pandemic. In fact, starting in the 12 months ending in January of 2013 and extending all the way up until the pandemic, the number of jobs added was drastically higher than what we have seen lately.

It's not just the total number of jobs that is concerning. It's also the fact that job openings are worsening. The most recent data that we have for this metric is from December of 2025. During that month, we saw only 6.54 million job openings. That's down 12.9% from the 7.51 million reported a year earlier. This is the lowest that we have seen since September of 2020. And if we ignore the time of the COVID-19 pandemic, you would have to go all the way back to December of 2017 to see a month when job openings were worse than this.

Takeaway

The way I see it, we have two different paths that lie ahead. The best one would be one in which tariffs are vanquished for good. This would immediately improve the condition for the vast majority of Americans. The other path is the one that we seem to be on. And this is one in which further attempts are made at imposing tariffs for the long haul. If this path is indeed the one we are destined for, it is my stance that economic conditions will continue to be fragile. The fact that the most extreme tariffs are now off the table, at least for the moment, makes me feel a bit more optimistic about the state of the economy. But until we see data improve drastically, or until we see a change in overall policy, I still maintain that a recession is a high-probability event right now.
